title: Basketball

infobox.value: 5 per side: [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard") [shooting guard](/wiki/Shooting_guard "Shooting guard") [small forward](/wiki/Small_forward "Small forward") [power forward](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward") [center](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)")
Yes, demonstrated in the 1904 and 1924 Summer Olympics Part of the Summer Olympic program since 1936 and [3x3 basketball](/wiki/3x3_basketball "3x3 basketball") since 2020

is a [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") in which two teams of five players each (excluding subs), opposing one another on a rectangular [court](/wiki/Basketball_court "Basketball court"), compete with the primary objective of [shooting](#Shooting) a [basketball](/wiki/Basketball_(ball) "Basketball (ball)") (approximately 9.4 inches (24 cm) in diameter) through the defender's [hoop](/wiki/Basket_(basketball) "Basket (basketball)") (a basket 18 inches (46 cm) in diameter mounted 10 feet (3.05 m) high to a [backboard](/wiki/Backboard_(basketball) "Backboard (basketball)") at each end of the court). Teams alternate between [offense](/wiki/Offense_(sports) "Offense (sports)"), when they attempt to score, and [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sports) "Defense (sports)"), when they try to prevent the opposing side from scoring. A [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal_(basketball) "Field goal (basketball)") is worth two points, unless made from behind the [three-point line](/wiki/3_point_line "3 point line"), when it is worth three. After a [foul](/wiki/Foul_(basketball) "Foul (basketball)"), timed play stops and the player fouled or designated to shoot a [technical foul](/wiki/Technical_foul "Technical foul") is given one, two or three one-point [free throws](/wiki/Free_throw "Free throw"). The team with the most points at the end of the game wins, but if regulation play expires with the score tied, an additional period of play ([overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)")) is mandated. However, if the additional period still results in a tied score, yet another additional period is mandated. This goes on until the score is not tied anymore.

**Basketball** is a [team sport](/wiki/Team_sport "Team sport") in which two teams of five players each (excluding subs), opposing one another on a rectangular [court](/wiki/Basketball_court "Basketball court"), compete with the primary objective of [shooting](#Shooting) a [basketball](/wiki/Basketball_(ball) "Basketball (ball)") (approximately 9.4 inches (24 cm) in diameter) through the defender's [hoop](/wiki/Basket_(basketball) "Basket (basketball)") (a basket 18 inches (46 cm) in diameter mounted 10 feet (3.05 m) high to a [backboard](/wiki/Backboard_(basketball) "Backboard (basketball)") at each end of the court). Teams alternate between [offense](/wiki/Offense_(sports) "Offense (sports)"), when they attempt to score, and [defense](/wiki/Defense_(sports) "Defense (sports)"), when they try to prevent the opposing side from scoring. A [field goal](/wiki/Field_goal_(basketball) "Field goal (basketball)") is worth two points, unless made from behind the [three-point line](/wiki/3_point_line "3 point line"), when it is worth three. After a [foul](/wiki/Foul_(basketball) "Foul (basketball)"), timed play stops and the player fouled or designated to shoot a [technical foul](/wiki/Technical_foul "Technical foul") is given one, two or three one-point [free throws](/wiki/Free_throw "Free throw"). The team with the most points at the end of the game wins, but if regulation play expires with the score tied, an additional period of play ([overtime](/wiki/Overtime_(sports) "Overtime (sports)")) is mandated. However, if the additional period still results in a tied score, yet another additional period is mandated. This goes on until the score is not tied anymore.

Players advance the ball by bouncing it while walking or running (dribbling) or by passing it to a teammate, both of which require considerable skill. On offense, players may use a variety of shots – the [layup](/wiki/Layup "Layup"), the [jump shot](/wiki/Jump_shot_(basketball) "Jump shot (basketball)"), or a [dunk](/wiki/Slam_dunk "Slam dunk"); on defense, they may [steal](/wiki/Steal_(basketball) "Steal (basketball)") the ball from a dribbler, intercept passes, or [block](/wiki/Block_(basketball) "Block (basketball)") shots; either offense or defense may collect a [rebound](/wiki/Rebound_(basketball) "Rebound (basketball)"), that is, a missed shot that bounces from rim or backboard. It is a violation to [lift or drag one's pivot foot without dribbling the ball](/wiki/Traveling_(basketball) "Traveling (basketball)"), to [carry](/wiki/Carrying_(basketball) "Carrying (basketball)") it, or to [hold the ball with both hands then resume dribbling](/wiki/Double_dribble "Double dribble").

The five players on each side fall into five [playing positions](/wiki/Basketball_positions "Basketball positions"). The tallest player is usually the [center](/wiki/Center_(basketball) "Center (basketball)"), the second-tallest and strongest is the [power forward](/wiki/Power_forward "Power forward"), a slightly shorter but more agile player is the [small forward](/wiki/Small_forward "Small forward"), and the shortest players or the best ball handlers are the [shooting guard](/wiki/Shooting_guard "Shooting guard") and the [point guard](/wiki/Point_guard "Point guard"), who implement the coach's game plan by managing the execution of offensive and defensive plays (player positioning). Informally, players may play three-on-three, two-on-two, and one-on-one.

Invented in 1891 by Canadian-American gym teacher [James Naismith](/wiki/James_Naismith "James Naismith") in [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"), in the United States, basketball has evolved to become one of the world's most popular and widely viewed sports.[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2) The [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) is the most significant [professional basketball](/wiki/Professional_basketball "Professional basketball") league in the world in terms of popularity, salaries, talent, and level of competition[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) (drawing most of its talent from U.S. [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball")). Outside North America, the top clubs from national leagues qualify to continental championships such as the [EuroLeague](/wiki/EuroLeague "EuroLeague") and the [Basketball Champions League Americas](/wiki/Basketball_Champions_League_Americas "Basketball Champions League Americas"). The [FIBA Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Basketball World Cup") and [Men's Olympic Basketball Tournament](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Summer_Olympics#Men's_tournament "Basketball at the Summer Olympics") are the major international events of the sport and attract top national teams from around the world. Each continent hosts regional competitions for national teams, like [EuroBasket](/wiki/EuroBasket "EuroBasket") and [FIBA AmeriCup](/wiki/FIBA_AmeriCup "FIBA AmeriCup").

The [FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup") and [women's Olympic basketball tournament](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Summer_Olympics#Women's_tournament "Basketball at the Summer Olympics") feature top national teams from continental championships. The main North American league is the [WNBA](/wiki/WNBA "WNBA") ([NCAA Women's Division I Basketball Championship](/wiki/NCAA_Women%27s_Division_I_Basketball_Championship "NCAA Women's Division I Basketball Championship") is also popular), whereas the strongest European clubs participate in the [EuroLeague Women](/wiki/EuroLeague_Women "EuroLeague Women").

## History

Main article: [History of basketball](/wiki/History_of_basketball "History of basketball")

### Early history

A game similar to basketball is mentioned in a 1591 book published in Frankfurt am Main that reports on the lifestyles and customs of coastal North American residents, *Wahrhafftige Abconterfaytung der Wilden* (German; translates as *Truthful Depictions of the Savages)*:[[5]](#cite_note-5) "Among other things, a game of skill is described in which balls must be thrown against a target woven from twigs, mounted high on a pole. There's a small reward for the player if the target is being hit."[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Creation

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/20/James_Naismith_at_Springfield_College_circa_1920.jpg/250px-James_Naismith_at_Springfield_College_circa_1920.jpg)](/wiki/File:James_Naismith_at_Springfield_College_circa_1920.jpg)

[James Naismith](/wiki/James_Naismith "James Naismith") c. 1920

[![An early manual for teaching basketball](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/68/How_to_play_basket_ball%3B_a_thesis_on_the_technique_of_the_game_%28IA_howtoplaybasketb02mess%29.pdf/page1-250px-How_to_play_basket_ball%3B_a_thesis_on_the_technique_of_the_game_%28IA_howtoplaybasketb02mess%29.pdf.jpg)](/wiki/File:How_to_play_basket_ball;_a_thesis_on_the_technique_of_the_game_(IA_howtoplaybasketb02mess).pdf)

An early manual for teaching basketball

In December 1891, [James Naismith](/wiki/James_Naismith "James Naismith"), a Canadian-American professor of [physical education](/wiki/Physical_education "Physical education") and instructor at the International [Young Men's Christian Association](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") Training School (now [Springfield College](/wiki/Springfield_College "Springfield College")) in [Springfield, Massachusetts](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"),[[7]](#cite_note-7) was trying to keep his gym class active on a rainy day.[[8]](#cite_note-8) He sought a vigorous indoor game to keep his students occupied and at proper levels of fitness during the long [New England](/wiki/New_England "New England") winters. After rejecting other ideas as either too rough or poorly suited to walled-in [gymnasiums](/wiki/Gymnasiums "Gymnasiums"), he invented a new game in which players would pass a ball to teammates and try to score points by tossing the ball into a basket mounted on a wall.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f8/Firstbasketball.jpg/250px-Firstbasketball.jpg)](/wiki/File:Firstbasketball.jpg)

The first basketball court at [Springfield College](/wiki/Springfield_College "Springfield College")

Naismith wrote the basic [rules](/wiki/Rules_of_basketball "Rules of basketball") and nailed a peach basket onto an elevated track. Naismith initially set up the peach basket with its bottom intact, which meant that the ball had to be retrieved manually after each "basket" or point scored. This quickly proved tedious, so Naismith removed the bottom of the basket to allow the balls to be poked out with a long [dowel](/wiki/Dowel "Dowel") after each scored basket.

Shortly after, [Senda Berenson](/wiki/Senda_Berenson_Abbott "Senda Berenson Abbott"), instructor of physical culture at the nearby [Smith College](/wiki/Smith_College "Smith College"), went to Naismith to learn more about the game.[[9]](#cite_note-Pioneers_in_Physical_Education-9) Fascinated by the new sport and the values it could teach, she started to organize games with her pupils, following adjusted rules.[[10]](#cite_note-Senda_Berenson_Papers-10) The first official women's interinstitutional game was played barely 11 months later, between the [University of California](/wiki/University_of_California,_Berkeley "University of California, Berkeley") and Miss Head's School.[[11]](#cite_note-Jenkins-11) In 1899, a committee was established at the Conference of Physical Training in Springfield to draw up general rules for women's basketball. Thus, the sport quickly spread throughout America's schools, colleges and universities with uniform rules for both sexes.[[12]](#cite_note-Berenson1901-12)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/31/Basketball_historic.tif/lossless-page1-250px-Basketball_historic.tif.png)](/wiki/File:Basketball_historic.tif)

The first basketballs used were association footballs

Basketball was originally played with a [soccer ball](/wiki/Ball_(association_football) "Ball (association football)"). These round balls from "[association football](/wiki/Association_football "Association football")" were made, at the time, with a set of laces to close off the hole needed for inserting the inflatable bladder after the other sewn-together segments of the ball's cover had been flipped outside-in.[[13]](#cite_note-13)[[14]](#cite_note-14) These laces could cause bounce passes and dribbling to be unpredictable.[[15]](#cite_note-15) Eventually a lace-free ball construction method was invented, and this change to the game was endorsed by Naismith (whereas in [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football"), the lace construction proved to be advantageous for gripping and remains to this day). The first balls made specifically for basketball were brown, and it was only in the late 1950s that [Tony Hinkle](/wiki/Paul_D._%22Tony%22_Hinkle "Paul D. \"Tony\" Hinkle"), searching for a ball that would be more visible to players and spectators alike, introduced the orange ball that is now in common use. Dribbling was not part of the original game except for the "bounce pass" to teammates. Passing the ball was the primary means of ball movement. Dribbling was eventually introduced but limited by the asymmetric shape of early balls.[*[dubious](/wiki/Wikipedia:Accuracy_dispute#Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Accuracy dispute") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Basketball#Dubious "Talk:Basketball")*] Dribbling was common by 1896, with a rule against the double dribble by 1898.[[16]](#cite_note-16)

The peach baskets were used until 1906 when they were finally replaced by metal hoops with backboards. A further change was soon made, so the ball merely passed through. Whenever a person got the ball in the basket, their team would gain a point. Whichever team got the most points won the game.[[17]](#cite_note-17) The baskets were originally nailed to the mezzanine balcony of the playing court, but this proved impractical when spectators in the balcony began to interfere with shots. The backboard was introduced to prevent this interference; it had the additional effect of allowing rebound shots.[[18]](#cite_note-18) Naismith's handwritten diaries, discovered by his granddaughter in early 2006, indicate that he was nervous about the new game he had invented, which incorporated rules from a children's game called [duck on a rock](/wiki/Duck_on_a_rock "Duck on a rock"), as many had failed before it.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

Frank Mahan, one of the players from the original first game, approached Naismith after the Christmas break, in early 1892, asking him what he intended to call his new game. Naismith replied that he had not thought of it because he had been focused on just getting the [game](/wiki/Game "Game") started. Mahan suggested that it be called "Naismith ball", at which he laughed, saying that a name like that would kill any game. Mahan then said, "Why not call it basketball?" Naismith replied, "We have a basket and a ball, and it seems to me that would be a good name for it."[[20]](#cite_note-20)[[21]](#cite_note-21) The first official game was played in the [YMCA gymnasium in Albany](/wiki/Young_Men%27s_Christian_Association_Building_(Albany,_New_York) "Young Men's Christian Association Building (Albany, New York)"), New York, on January 20, 1892, with nine players. The game ended at 1–0; the shot was made from 25 feet (7.6 m), on a court just half the size of a present-day [Streetball](/wiki/Streetball "Streetball") or [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA) court.

At the time, [soccer](/wiki/Association_football "Association football") was being played with 10 to a team (which was increased to 11). When winter weather got too icy to play soccer, teams were taken indoors, and it was convenient to have them split in half and play basketball with five on each side. By 1897–98, teams of five became standard.

### College basketball

Main article: [College basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/5e/Kansas_U_team_1899.jpg/250px-Kansas_U_team_1899.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kansas_U_team_1899.jpg)

The 1899 University of Kansas basketball team, with James Naismith at the back, right

Basketball's early adherents were dispatched to YMCAs throughout the United States, and it quickly spread through the United States and Canada. By 1895, it was well established at several women's high schools. While YMCA was responsible for initially developing and spreading the game, within a decade it discouraged the new sport, as rough play and rowdy crowds began to detract from YMCA's primary mission. However, other amateur sports clubs, colleges, and professional clubs quickly filled the void. In the years before [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), the [Amateur Athletic Union](/wiki/Amateur_Athletic_Union "Amateur Athletic Union") and the [Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States](/wiki/Intercollegiate_Athletic_Association_of_the_United_States "Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States") (forerunner of the [NCAA](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association")) vied for control over the rules for the game. The first pro league, the National Basketball League, was formed in 1898 to protect players from exploitation and to promote a less rough game. This league only lasted five years.

James Naismith was instrumental in establishing [college basketball](/wiki/College_basketball "College basketball"). His colleague C. O. Beamis fielded the first college basketball team just a year after the Springfield YMCA game at the suburban Pittsburgh [Geneva College](/wiki/Geneva_College "Geneva College").[[22]](#cite_note-22) Naismith himself later coached at the [University of Kansas](/wiki/Kansas_Jayhawks_men%27s_basketball "Kansas Jayhawks men's basketball") for six years, before handing the reins to renowned coach [Forrest "Phog" Allen](/wiki/Phog_Allen "Phog Allen"). Naismith's disciple [Amos Alonzo Stagg](/wiki/Amos_Alonzo_Stagg "Amos Alonzo Stagg") brought basketball to the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), while [Adolph Rupp](/wiki/Adolph_Rupp "Adolph Rupp"), a student of Naismith's at Kansas, enjoyed great success as coach at the [University of Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky_Wildcats_men%27s_basketball "Kentucky Wildcats men's basketball"). On February 9, 1895, the first intercollegiate 5-on-5 game was played at [Hamline University](/wiki/Hamline_University "Hamline University") between Hamline and the School of Agriculture, which was affiliated with the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota").[[23]](#cite_note-23)[[24]](#cite_note-24)[[25]](#cite_note-25) The School of Agriculture won in a 9–3 game.

In 1901, colleges, including the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago"), [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University"), [Cornell University](/wiki/Cornell_University "Cornell University"), [Dartmouth College](/wiki/Dartmouth_College "Dartmouth College"), the [University of Minnesota](/wiki/University_of_Minnesota "University of Minnesota"), the [U.S. Naval Academy](/wiki/United_States_Naval_Academy "United States Naval Academy"), the [University of Colorado](/wiki/University_of_Colorado_Boulder "University of Colorado Boulder") and [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University") began sponsoring men's games. In 1905, frequent injuries on the [football](/wiki/College_football "College football") field prompted President [Theodore Roosevelt](/wiki/Theodore_Roosevelt "Theodore Roosevelt") to suggest that colleges form a governing body, resulting in the creation of the Intercollegiate Athletic Association of the United States (IAAUS). In 1910, that body changed its name to the [National Collegiate Athletic Association](/wiki/National_Collegiate_Athletic_Association "National Collegiate Athletic Association") (NCAA). The first Canadian interuniversity basketball game was played at [YMCA](/wiki/YMCA "YMCA") in [Kingston](/wiki/Kingston,_Ontario "Kingston, Ontario"), Ontario on February 6, 1904, when [McGill University](/wiki/McGill_University "McGill University") – Naismith's alma mater – visited [Queen's University](/wiki/Queen%27s_University_at_Kingston "Queen's University at Kingston"). McGill won 9–7 in overtime; the score was 7–7 at the end of regulation play, and a ten-minute overtime period settled the outcome. A good turnout of spectators watched the game.[[26]](#cite_note-26)

The first men's national championship tournament, the National Association of Intercollegiate Basketball tournament, which still exists as the [National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics](/wiki/National_Association_of_Intercollegiate_Athletics "National Association of Intercollegiate Athletics") (NAIA) [tournament](/wiki/NAIA_national_men%27s_basketball_championship "NAIA national men's basketball championship"), was organized in 1937. The first national championship for NCAA teams, the [National Invitation Tournament](/wiki/National_Invitation_Tournament "National Invitation Tournament") (NIT) in New York, was organized in 1938; the [NCAA national tournament](/wiki/NCAA_Division_I_men%27s_basketball_tournament "NCAA Division I men's basketball tournament") began one year later. College basketball was rocked by gambling scandals from 1948 to [1951](/wiki/CCNY_point_shaving_scandal "CCNY point shaving scandal"), when dozens of players from top teams were implicated in [game-fixing](/wiki/Match_fixing "Match fixing") and [point shaving](/wiki/Point_shaving "Point shaving"). Partially spurred by an association with cheating, the NIT lost support to the NCAA tournament.

### High school basketball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ee/Heart_Mountain_Relocation_Center%2C_Heart_Mountain%2C_Wyoming._A_hotly_contested_interscholastic_basket_._._._-_NARA_-_539726.jpg/250px-Heart_Mountain_Relocation_Center%2C_Heart_Mountain%2C_Wyoming._A_hotly_contested_interscholastic_basket_._._._-_NARA_-_539726.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heart_Mountain_Relocation_Center,_Heart_Mountain,_Wyoming._A_hotly_contested_interscholastic_basket_._._._-_NARA_-_539726.jpg)

A basketball game between the [Heart Mountain](/wiki/Heart_Mountain_Relocation_Center "Heart Mountain Relocation Center") and Powell High School girls teams, Wyoming, March 1944

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/HS_Basketball_2024.jpg/250px-HS_Basketball_2024.jpg)](/wiki/File:HS_Basketball_2024.jpg)

A photo taken during a high school basketball game in 2024. Demonstrates the extreme disparities in terms of physical maturity that can be seen between opposing players.

Before widespread [school district](/wiki/School_district "School district") consolidation, most American high schools were far smaller than their present-day counterparts. During the first decades of the 20th century, basketball quickly became the ideal interscholastic sport due to its modest equipment and personnel requirements. In the days before widespread television coverage of professional and college sports, the popularity of [high school basketball](/wiki/High_school_basketball "High school basketball") was unrivaled in many parts of America. Perhaps the most legendary of high school teams was Indiana's [Franklin Wonder Five](/wiki/Franklin_Wonder_Five "Franklin Wonder Five"), which took the nation by storm during the 1920s, dominating Indiana basketball and earning national recognition.

Today virtually every high school in the United States fields a basketball team in [varsity](/wiki/Varsity_team "Varsity team") competition.[[27]](#cite_note-27) Basketball's popularity remains high, both in rural areas where they carry the identification of the entire community, as well as at some larger schools known for their basketball teams where many players go on to participate at higher levels of competition after graduation. In the 2016–17 season, 980,673 boys and girls represented their schools in interscholastic basketball competition, according to the [National Federation of State High School Associations](/wiki/National_Federation_of_State_High_School_Associations "National Federation of State High School Associations").[[28]](#cite_note-28) The states of [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana") and [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky") are particularly well known for their residents' devotion to high school basketball, commonly called [Hoosier Hysteria](/wiki/Hoosier_Hysteria "Hoosier Hysteria") in Indiana; the critically acclaimed film *[Hoosiers](/wiki/Hoosiers_(film) "Hoosiers (film)")* shows high school basketball's depth of meaning to these communities.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ac/The_High_School_Magazine._Montreal%2C_Canada_%281916%29_%2814779954132%29.jpg/250px-The_High_School_Magazine._Montreal%2C_Canada_%281916%29_%2814779954132%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_High_School_Magazine._Montreal,_Canada_(1916)_(14779954132).jpg)

[High School of Montreal](/wiki/High_School_of_Montreal "High School of Montreal") Girls Junior Basketball team, 1915–1916

⁣There is currently no tournament to determine a national high school champion. The most serious effort was the National Interscholastic Basketball Tournament at the [University of Chicago](/wiki/University_of_Chicago "University of Chicago") from 1917 to 1930. The event was organized by [Amos Alonzo Stagg](/wiki/Amos_Alonzo_Stagg "Amos Alonzo Stagg") and sent invitations to state champion teams. The tournament started out as a mostly Midwest affair but grew. In 1929 it had 29 state champions. Faced with opposition from the [National Federation of State High School Associations](/wiki/National_Federation_of_State_High_School_Associations "National Federation of State High School Associations") and [North Central Association of Colleges and Schools](/wiki/North_Central_Association_of_Colleges_and_Schools "North Central Association of Colleges and Schools") that bore a threat of the schools losing their accreditation the last tournament was in 1930. The organizations said they were concerned that the tournament was being used to recruit professional players from the prep ranks.[[29]](#cite_note-29) The tournament did not invite minority schools or private/parochial schools.

The National Catholic Interscholastic Basketball Tournament ran from 1924 to 1941 at [Loyola University](/wiki/Loyola_University_Chicago "Loyola University Chicago").[[30]](#cite_note-30) The National Catholic Invitational Basketball Tournament from 1954 to 1978 played at a series of venues, including [Catholic University](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_America "Catholic University of America"), [Georgetown](/wiki/Georgetown_University "Georgetown University") and [George Mason](/wiki/George_Mason_University "George Mason University").[[31]](#cite_note-31) The National Interscholastic Basketball Tournament for Black High Schools was held from 1929 to 1942 at [Hampton Institute](/wiki/Hampton_University "Hampton University").[[32]](#cite_note-32) The National Invitational Interscholastic Basketball Tournament was held from 1941 to 1967 starting out at [Tuskegee Institute](/wiki/Tuskegee_University "Tuskegee University"). Following a pause during [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") it resumed at [Tennessee State College](/wiki/Tennessee_State_University "Tennessee State University") in Nashville. The basis for the champion dwindled after 1954 when *[Brown v. Board of Education](/wiki/Brown_v._Board_of_Education "Brown v. Board of Education")* began an integration of schools. The last tournaments were held at [Alabama State College](/wiki/Alabama_State_University "Alabama State University") from 1964 to 1967.[[33]](#cite_note-33)

### Professional basketball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/en/thumb/e/e2/Liberator-ad.jpg/250px-Liberator-ad.jpg)](/wiki/File:Liberator-ad.jpg)

Ad from *[The Liberator](/wiki/The_Liberator_(magazine) "The Liberator (magazine)")* magazine promoting an exhibition in Harlem, March 1922. Drawing by [Hugo Gellert](/wiki/Hugo_Gellert "Hugo Gellert")

Teams abounded throughout the 1920s. There were hundreds of men's [professional basketball](/wiki/Professional_basketball "Professional basketball") teams in towns and cities all over the United States, and little organization of the professional game. Players jumped from team to team and teams played in armories and smoky dance halls. Leagues came and went. [Barnstorming](/wiki/Barnstorm_(sports) "Barnstorm (sports)") squads such as the [Original Celtics](/wiki/Original_Celtics "Original Celtics") and two all-African American teams, the [New York Renaissance Five](/wiki/New_York_Renaissance "New York Renaissance") ("Rens") and the (still existing) [Harlem Globetrotters](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters") played up to two hundred games a year on their national tours.

In 1946, the [Basketball Association of America](/wiki/Basketball_Association_of_America "Basketball Association of America") (BAA) was formed. The first game was played in [Toronto](/wiki/Toronto "Toronto"), Ontario, Canada between the [Toronto Huskies](/wiki/Toronto_Huskies "Toronto Huskies") and [New York Knickerbockers](/wiki/New_York_Knicks "New York Knicks") on November 1, 1946. Three seasons later, in 1949, the BAA merged with the [National Basketball League](/wiki/National_Basketball_League_(United_States) "National Basketball League (United States)") (NBL) to form the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA). By the 1950s, basketball had become a major college sport, thus paving the way for a growth of interest in professional basketball. In 1959, a [basketball hall of fame](/wiki/Naismith_Memorial_Basketball_Hall_of_Fame "Naismith Memorial Basketball Hall of Fame") was founded in [Springfield](/wiki/Springfield,_Massachusetts "Springfield, Massachusetts"), Massachusetts, site of the first game. Its rosters include the names of great players, coaches, referees and people who have contributed significantly to the development of the game. The hall of fame has people who have accomplished many goals in their career in basketball. An upstart organization, the [American Basketball Association](/wiki/American_Basketball_Association "American Basketball Association"), emerged in 1967 and briefly threatened the NBA's dominance until the [ABA-NBA merger](/wiki/ABA-NBA_merger "ABA-NBA merger") in 1976. Today the NBA is the top professional basketball league in the world in terms of popularity, salaries, talent, and level of competition.

[Diamond DeShields](/wiki/Diamond_DeShields "Diamond DeShields") (#1) drives to the basket around [Maya Moore](/wiki/Maya_Moore "Maya Moore") (#23) in the Minnesota Lynx vs Chicago Sky game

The NBA has featured many famous players, including [George Mikan](/wiki/George_Mikan "George Mikan"), the first dominating "big man"; ball-handling wizard [Bob Cousy](/wiki/Bob_Cousy "Bob Cousy") and defensive genius [Bill Russell](/wiki/Bill_Russell "Bill Russell") of the [Boston Celtics](/wiki/Boston_Celtics "Boston Celtics"); charismatic center [Wilt Chamberlain](/wiki/Wilt_Chamberlain "Wilt Chamberlain"), who originally played for the barnstorming [Harlem Globetrotters](/wiki/Harlem_Globetrotters "Harlem Globetrotters"); all-around stars [Oscar Robertson](/wiki/Oscar_Robertson "Oscar Robertson") and [Jerry West](/wiki/Jerry_West "Jerry West"); more recent big men [Kareem Abdul-Jabbar](/wiki/Kareem_Abdul-Jabbar "Kareem Abdul-Jabbar"), [Shaquille O'Neal](/wiki/Shaquille_O%27Neal "Shaquille O'Neal"), [Hakeem Olajuwon](/wiki/Hakeem_Olajuwon "Hakeem Olajuwon") and [Karl Malone](/wiki/Karl_Malone "Karl Malone"); playmakers [John Stockton](/wiki/John_Stockton "John Stockton"), [Isiah Thomas](/wiki/Isiah_Thomas "Isiah Thomas") and [Steve Nash](/wiki/Steve_Nash "Steve Nash"); crowd-pleasing forwards [Julius Erving](/wiki/Julius_Erving "Julius Erving") and [Charles Barkley](/wiki/Charles_Barkley "Charles Barkley"); European stars [Dirk Nowitzki](/wiki/Dirk_Nowitzki "Dirk Nowitzki"), [Pau Gasol](/wiki/Pau_Gasol "Pau Gasol"), [Nikola Jokić](/wiki/Nikola_Joki%C4%87 "Nikola Jokić") and [Tony Parker](/wiki/Tony_Parker "Tony Parker"); Latin American stars [Manu Ginobili](/wiki/Manu_Ginobili "Manu Ginobili"), more recent superstars, [Allen Iverson](/wiki/Allen_Iverson "Allen Iverson"), [Kobe Bryant](/wiki/Kobe_Bryant "Kobe Bryant"), [Tim Duncan](/wiki/Tim_Duncan "Tim Duncan"), [LeBron James](/wiki/LeBron_James "LeBron James"), [Stephen Curry](/wiki/Stephen_Curry "Stephen Curry"), [Giannis Antetokounmpo](/wiki/Giannis_Antetokounmpo "Giannis Antetokounmpo"), etc.; and the three players who many credit with ushering the professional game to its highest level of popularity during the 1980s and 1990s: [Larry Bird](/wiki/Larry_Bird "Larry Bird"), [Earvin "Magic" Johnson](/wiki/Magic_Johnson "Magic Johnson"), and [Michael Jordan](/wiki/Michael_Jordan "Michael Jordan").

In 2001, the NBA formed a developmental league, the [National Basketball Development League](/wiki/National_Basketball_Development_League "National Basketball Development League") (later known as the NBA D-League and then the [NBA G League](/wiki/NBA_G_League "NBA G League") after a branding deal with [Gatorade](/wiki/Gatorade "Gatorade")). As of the 2023–24 season, the G League has 31 teams.

### International basketball

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/96/Basketball_World_Cup_2014.jpg/250px-Basketball_World_Cup_2014.jpg)](/wiki/File:Basketball_World_Cup_2014.jpg)

The U.S. playing against Mexico at the [2014 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIBA_World_Cup "2014 FIBA World Cup")

[FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") (International Basketball Federation) was formed in 1932 by eight founding nations: [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina "Argentina"), [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia"), [Greece](/wiki/Greece "Greece"), [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy"), [Latvia](/wiki/Latvia "Latvia"), [Portugal](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal"), [Romania](/wiki/Romania "Romania") and [Switzerland](/wiki/Switzerland "Switzerland"). At this time, the organization only oversaw amateur players. Its acronym, derived from the French *Fédération Internationale de Basket-ball Amateur*, was thus "FIBA". Men's basketball was first [included](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the Summer Olympics") at the Berlin [1936 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1936_Summer_Olympics "1936 Summer Olympics"), although a demonstration tournament was held in 1904. The United States defeated Canada in the first final, played outdoors. This competition has usually been dominated by the United States, whose team has won all but three titles. The first of these came in a controversial final game in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich") in [1972](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_1972_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 1972 Summer Olympics") against the Soviet Union, in which the ending of the game was replayed three times until the Soviet Union finally came out on top.[[34]](#cite_note-Bloomberg-34) In 1950 the first FIBA World Championship for men, now known as the [FIBA Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Basketball World Cup"), was held in Argentina. Three years later, the first FIBA World Championship for women, now known as the [FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup](/wiki/FIBA_Women%27s_Basketball_World_Cup "FIBA Women's Basketball World Cup"), was held in Chile. Women's basketball was added to the Olympics in 1976, which were held in [Montreal](/wiki/Montreal "Montreal"), Quebec, Canada with teams such as the [Soviet Union](/wiki/Soviet_Union "Soviet Union"), [Brazil](/wiki/Brazil_women%27s_national_basketball_team "Brazil women's national basketball team") and [Australia](/wiki/Australia_women%27s_national_basketball_team "Australia women's national basketball team") rivaling the [American](/wiki/United_States_women%27s_national_basketball_team "United States women's national basketball team") squads.

In 1989, [FIBA](/wiki/FIBA "FIBA") allowed professional NBA players to participate in the [Olympics](/wiki/Olympics "Olympics") for the first time. Prior to the [1992 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1992_Summer_Olympics "1992 Summer Olympics"), only European and South American teams were allowed to field professionals in the Olympics. The United States' dominance continued with the introduction of the original [Dream Team](/wiki/1992_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "1992 United States men's Olympic basketball team"). In the [2004 Athens Olympics](/wiki/2004_Summer_Olympics "2004 Summer Olympics"), the United States suffered its first Olympic loss while using professional players, falling to [Puerto Rico](/wiki/Puerto_Rico_national_basketball_team "Puerto Rico national basketball team") (in a 19-point loss) and [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania_men%27s_national_basketball_team "Lithuania men's national basketball team") in group games, and being eliminated in the semifinals by [Argentina](/wiki/Argentina_national_basketball_team "Argentina national basketball team"). It eventually won the bronze medal defeating Lithuania, finishing behind Argentina and [Italy](/wiki/Italy_national_basketball_team "Italy national basketball team"). The [Redeem Team](/wiki/2008_United_States_men%27s_Olympic_basketball_team "2008 United States men's Olympic basketball team"), won gold at the [2008 Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 2008 Summer Olympics"), and the B-Team, won gold at the [2010 FIBA World Championship](/wiki/2010_FIBA_World_Championship "2010 FIBA World Championship") in Turkey despite featuring no players from the 2008 squad. The United States continued its dominance as they won gold at the [2012 Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 2012 Summer Olympics"), [2014 FIBA World Cup](/wiki/2014_FIBA_Basketball_World_Cup "2014 FIBA Basketball World Cup") and the [2016 Olympics](/wiki/Basketball_at_the_2016_Summer_Olympics "Basketball at the 2016 Summer Olympics").
